input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin (PT)** system under study is a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)-based incubator**, designed for temperature regulation within an insulated container. The system operates in the **thermal control domain** and comprises the following key components:
  - **Insulated Container**: A styrofoam box providing thermal insulation to maintain internal temperature stability.
  - **Heating Element**: A **heatbed** responsible for generating and distributing heat inside the container.
  - **Air Circulation**: A **fan** to ensure uniform temperature distribution within the box.
  - **Sensors**:
    - **Two internal temperature sensors** for real-time monitoring of the internal environment.
    - **One external temperature sensor** to measure ambient conditions outside the container.
  - **Control Unit**: A **Raspberry Pi** acting as the central controller for closed-loop regulation of temperature.
  The system is **suitable for digital twinning** due to its **dynamic, data-driven operational nature**, requiring real-time monitoring, predictive modeling, and adaptive control to maintain performance despite environmental variations (e.g., external temperature fluctuations or internal load changes).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** of the incubator provides the following **specific services** to users and the physical system, leveraging **modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimization** technologies:
  - **Real-Time Monitoring**: Continuous acquisition of **sensor data** (internal/external temperatures) via **time-series data collection**, enabling **state estimation** of the physical system.
  - **Dynamic Simulation**: Execution of **physics-based models** (e.g., differential equations) to **reproduce and predict** the incubator’s behavior under current or hypothetical operating conditions, including **thermal dynamics** and **control loop responses**.
  - **Calibration & Model Update**: Use of **calibration algorithms** to refine **cyber-physical models** (e.g., thermal transfer models) by comparing simulated outputs with real-time sensor data, ensuring **parameter accuracy** over time.
  - **Safety & Performance Validation**: **Monitoring algorithms** (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers) to assess whether the system operates **safely and optimally**, triggering alerts or corrective actions (e.g., adjusting heating power) when deviations occur.
  - **Predictive Analytics**: Generation of **predictive insights** (e.g., temperature trends, failure probabilities) to enable **proactive maintenance** and **adaptive control strategies**.
  - **Visualization**: **Advanced visualizations** of system states (e.g., temperature gradients, control signals) for **engineers and operators**, facilitating decision-making.
  - **Reconfigurability & Robustness**: Enables **dynamic adaptation** to changing environments (e.g., external temperature shifts) by adjusting **control policies** or **model parameters** via **optimization algorithms**.
  **Target Users**:
  - **Engineers** (for design validation, fault diagnosis, and optimization).
  - **Operators** (for real-time monitoring and intervention).
  - **Maintenance Teams** (for predictive maintenance scheduling).
  **Service Capabilities**:
  - **Closed-loop control integration** (e.g., supervisory controllers interfacing with the Raspberry Pi).
  - **Multi-fidelity modeling** (e.g., combining high-fidelity thermal models with simplified control logic).
  - **Automated decision support** (e.g., triggering corrective actions via algorithmic or human-in-the-loop processes).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin implementation** for the incubator leverages the following **specific tools, technologies, and enablers**, as inferred from the case study and general DT frameworks:
  - **Hardware Platform**:
    - **Raspberry Pi** (embedded controller for real-time data acquisition and actuation).
    - **Sensor Network**: Temperature sensors (e.g., **DS18B20 or equivalent**) for internal/external measurements, interfaced via **I2C/SPI protocols**.
  - **Software & Development Tools**:
    - **Modeling Frameworks**:
      - **Physics-based modeling** (e.g., **differential equation solvers** like SciPy or MATLAB/Simulink for thermal dynamics).
      - **Calibration algorithms** (e.g., **parameter estimation techniques** such as least-squares or Bayesian inference to update model parameters).
    - **Simulation Engines**:
      - **Time-domain simulators** (e.g., **Python-based tools like Pyomo or custom scripts**) for replicating incubator behavior under varying conditions.
    - **Data Management**:
      - **Time-series databases** (e.g., **InfluxDB or SQLite**) for storing sensor data and simulation logs.
      - **Data preprocessing libraries** (e.g., **Pandas, NumPy**) for cleaning and feature extraction.
    - **Control & Optimization**:
      - **Supervisory controllers** (e.g., **PID tuning tools or MPC controllers** for temperature regulation).
      - **Optimization algorithms** (e.g., **gradient descent, genetic algorithms**) for refining control policies or model parameters.
    - **Visualization Tools**:
      - **Real-time dashboards** (e.g., **Grafana, Matplotlib**) for displaying temperature profiles, control signals, and alerts.
      - **3D/2D schematics** (e.g., **CAD models or interactive plots**) for system visualization.
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **Cyber-Physical Integration**: **Middleware** (e.g., **MQTT, ROS**) for seamless communication between sensors, controllers, and simulation modules.
    - **Cloud/Edge Hybrid Deployment**: Potential use of **edge computing** (e.g., Raspberry Pi local processing) to reduce latency, with optional **cloud-based analytics** for complex simulations.
  - **Open-Source Repository**:
    - **INTO-CPS Association’s Example Incubator** ([GitHub](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) provides **hardware/software specifications**, **model implementations**, and **experimental setups** for reproducibility.
  **Key Enablers**:
  - **Modularity**: Separation of **data collection**, **modeling**, **simulation**, and **control** layers for maintainability.
  - **Real-Time Capabilities**: Low-latency data acquisition and actuation to support **closed-loop control**.
  - **Interoperability**: Standardized interfaces (e.g., **OPC UA, REST APIs**) for integrating third-party tools.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin (DT) system in the incubator case study operates within a **centralized architecture** for the specific use case, where a single DT instance is responsible for integrating **data** (collected via sensors, e.g., two internal and one external temperature sensors), **models** (e.g., calibrated dynamic models of the Cyber-Physical System (CPS) and environment), and **algorithms** (e.g., calibration algorithms, fault detectors, or supervisory controllers). The document does not explicitly describe **multiple DT instances** or **decentralized architectures**, but the schematic (Fig. 1) implies a **hierarchical structure** where the DT orchestrates real-time monitoring and decision-making for the Physical Twin (PT). The **scope of responsibility** for the DT instance includes **parameter calibration** (updating models via algorithms) and **safety/optimality monitoring** (via monitors), with potential human or algorithmic intervention for corrective actions. No explicit mention of **coordination mechanisms** between multiple twins is provided, as the focus is on a single DT-PT pair for the incubator system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin (DT) system follows a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)-based architecture** (as depicted in Fig. 1), where the **orchestration** is structured around three core components: **data** (sensor-collected from the PT), **models** (representing both cyber and physical aspects of the PT and its environment), and **algorithms** (e.g., calibration, fault detection, or state estimation). The **coordination mechanism** involves:
  1. **Calibration algorithms** that update the DT’s models of the CPS and environment using real-time sensor data to refine parameter estimates.
  2. **Monitors** that evaluate the PT’s performance against safety and optimality criteria, triggering alerts or corrective actions (via human or automated decision-making).
  The **resource management** is implicit in the integration of these components, with no explicit mention of distributed resource allocation or dynamic reconfiguration. The **architecture pattern** is **closed-loop**, where the DT continuously synchronizes with the PT to maintain alignment between the digital and physical domains. The document does not specify **integration patterns** beyond the CPS framework or **system-wide coordination** for multi-DT scenarios.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) integrates **computational models** and **data components** to represent and analyze the behavior of the Physical Twin (PT). The models include:
  - **Geometric/Structural Models**: Abstract representations of physical components (e.g., CAD models of a car’s chassis, wheels, or an incubator’s insulated box), capturing static attributes like dimensions, shapes, and material properties.
  - **Behavioral/Dynamic Models**: Mathematical formulations (e.g., differential equations) describing system dynamics, such as forces acting on a car or thermal inertia in an incubator’s heatbed. The incubator case study employed **two simulation models**—one with **two free parameters** and another with **four free parameters**—to predict internal temperature, with the latter demonstrating superior accuracy in modeling thermal inertia.
  - **Analytical Models**: Algorithmic representations (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimizers) that process data and models to generate actionable insights or corrections.
  **Data Sources** include real-time sensor inputs from the PT (e.g., temperature, force, or environmental variables) and historical operational data. **Data Management** involves:
  - **Calibration Algorithms**: Continuously update model parameters (e.g., thermal inertia coefficients) using sensor data to minimize prediction errors.
  - **Model Relationships**: Hierarchical or modular linkages between cyber (computational) and physical (PT) models, enabling cross-domain validation (e.g., comparing simulated vs. monitored temperature in the incubator).
  The DT framework leverages these components to **reproduce, predict, and optimize** PT behavior under current or hypothetical conditions, with models acting as the bridge between raw data and high-level decision-making (e.g., triggering corrective actions via controllers).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The Digital Twin’s **physical acting components** in a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) include **actuators and control mechanisms** that interface with the Physical Twin (PT) to enforce adjustments or interventions. Key aspects include:
  - **Types of Actuators**: Mechanical (e.g., motors, solenoids), thermal (e.g., heatbeds in incubators), or fluidic components (e.g., valves in HVAC systems), selected based on the PT’s operational requirements (e.g., the incubator’s heatbed adjusts temperature via resistive heating).
  - **Control Capabilities**: Implemented via **Electronic Control Units (ECUs)** or embedded systems (e.g., a car’s ECUs communicate through a **Controller Area Network (CAN)** to coordinate actuators like throttle or braking systems). In the incubator case, a **software controller** processes simulation outputs to regulate the heatbed’s power.
  - **Remote Control Interfaces**: Networked communication protocols (e.g., CAN, Ethernet, or IoT standards) enable centralized or distributed control, allowing human operators or automated algorithms to override default settings (e.g., adjusting incubator temperature via a remote dashboard).
  - **Actuation Ranges/Limitations**: Defined by hardware constraints (e.g., a heatbed’s maximum power output or a car’s engine torque limits). Safety constraints (e.g., thermal cutoffs in incubators or anti-lock braking systems in cars) prevent PT damage or failure.
  - **Addressing Mechanisms**: Unique identifiers (e.g., IP addresses, CAN IDs) route control commands to specific actuators, ensuring precise targeting (e.g., isolating a faulty sensor’s calibration update to its corresponding model).
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The **sensing infrastructure** of a Digital Twin comprises **sensors** and data acquisition systems that monitor the Physical Twin (PT) and its environment in real time. Key specifications include:
  - **Sensor Types**: Dependent on the PT’s domain (e.g., **thermocouples** for temperature in incubators, **IMU sensors** for vehicle dynamics in cars, or **pressure sensors** for fluid systems). The incubator case study relies on **temperature sensors** to measure internal/external thermal conditions.
  - **Measurement Capabilities**: Quantify physical properties (e.g., temperature range: 0–100°C in incubators; acceleration: ±2g in vehicles) with resolutions tailored to model fidelity (e.g., high-precision sensors for thermal inertia modeling).
  - **Spatial Distribution**: Strategically placed to cover critical PT regions (e.g., multiple thermocouples in an incubator’s heatbed and walls to account for spatial temperature gradients). In vehicles, sensors may be distributed across the chassis, engine bay, and wheels.
  - **Sampling Frequencies**: Determined by dynamic system requirements (e.g., **millisecond-level sampling** for high-speed vehicle dynamics; **second-level sampling** for thermal systems like incubators). The incubator’s case study does not specify exact frequencies but implies continuous monitoring for model calibration.
  - **Data Transmission Rates**: Bandwidth-dependent protocols (e.g., **CAN bus** for automotive systems at 500 kbps; **Ethernet/IP** for industrial applications at 100 Mbps) ensure low-latency data delivery to the DT’s processing units.
  - **Accuracy/Precision Specifications**: Sensor tolerances (e.g., ±0.1°C for thermocouples) directly impact model validity. The incubator’s **four-parameter model** required higher-precision sensors to accurately capture thermal inertia.
  - **Data Collection Mechanisms**: Sensor data is aggregated via **embedded systems** (e.g., microcontrollers or PLCs) and transmitted to the DT’s **data layer**, where it undergoes preprocessing (e.g., noise filtering, normalization) before integration into models or algorithms.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
Ensuring **model fidelity** and **validity** in a Digital Twin involves rigorous **validation/verification (V&V) methods**, **uncertainty quantification**, and **quality assurance (QA)** processes. Key considerations include:
  - **Model Fidelity Requirements**: Defined by the PT’s criticality and operational context. The incubator case study demonstrated that a **four-parameter thermal model** (vs. a two-parameter model) achieved higher fidelity by better capturing the **thermal inertia of the heatbed**, a key factor for accurate temperature prediction. Fidelity is also tied to **spatial/temporal resolution** (e.g., granular sensor grids or high-frequency sampling).
  - **Validation and Verification Methods**:
    - **Simulation Validation**: Comparing DT predictions against real-world PT data (e.g., incubator temperature logs) to quantify errors. The case study used **experimental validation** to select the superior four-parameter model.
    - **Model Verification**: Ensuring mathematical consistency (e.g., differential equation solvers, parameter bounds) and adherence to physical laws (e.g., energy conservation in thermal systems).
    - **Cross-Validation**: Testing models under varied conditions (e.g., different ambient temperatures or PT configurations) to assess robustness.
  - **Uncertainty Sources and Quantification**:
    - **Sensor Noise**: Random errors in measurements (e.g., thermocouple drift) are mitigated via **Kalman filters** or moving averages.
    - **Model Parameter Uncertainty**: Addressed through **Bayesian inference** or **optimization algorithms** (e.g., minimizing the difference between simulated and observed temperatures in the incubator).
    - **Environmental Variability**: Uncontrolled factors (e.g., wind in a car’s PT or external heat sources in an incubator) are modeled as stochastic variables or bounded ranges.
  - **Error Detection and Correction Mechanisms**:
    - **Anomaly Detection**: Algorithms (e.g., **machine learning classifiers** or **statistical thresholds**) flag deviations (e.g., sudden temperature spikes in the incubator).
    - **Automatic Recalibration**: Triggered when prediction errors exceed thresholds, updating model parameters (e.g., adjusting thermal conductivity coefficients in the incubator’s model).
  - **Quality Assurance Processes**:
    - **Traceability**: Documenting model evolution (e.g., versioning simulation scripts or sensor calibration logs).
    - **Compliance Checks**: Ensuring DT outputs meet regulatory standards (e.g., ISO 26262 for automotive safety) or operational constraints (e.g., incubator temperature limits for biological samples).
    - **Continuous Monitoring**: Real-time **health checks** on sensors, actuators, and algorithms to preempt failures (e.g., detecting a faulty thermocouple before it corrupts the DT’s thermal model).
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Digital Twin (DT) of the incubator system collects **real-time sensor data** from the Physical Twin (PT) via **two temperature sensors inside the insulated styrofoam box** and **one temperature sensor outside the box**. The data types transmitted include **analog temperature readings** (likely in °C or °F) with unspecified precision but assumed to be high-resolution given the control requirements. The communication protocol is **not explicitly specified** in the documents, but the system implies a **direct wired or wireless connection** (e.g., via Raspberry Pi GPIO or serial/USB interfaces) to the cyber-physical system (CPS) controller. **Transmission frequency** is not detailed, but the context suggests **periodic polling** (e.g., every 1–10 seconds) to ensure real-time monitoring of temperature dynamics. **Data quality assurance** is implied through **calibration algorithms** (as referenced in Document 1, Fig. 1) that update the DT models with the best parameter estimates, ensuring accuracy. **Preprocessing steps** include raw data filtering (e.g., noise reduction) and **integration with DT models** via calibration techniques (e.g., least-squares fitting or Bayesian inference) to reconcile sensor readings with simulated behavior. The data is then used to inform **monitors** for safety and optimality checks, as described in Document 1.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Digital Twin (DT) exerts **control commands** to the Physical Twin (PT) via the **Raspberry Pi controller**, which acts as the interface between the cyber and physical layers. The **command types** include **temperature setpoints** and **actuation signals** (e.g., heating element activation/deactivation, fan speed adjustments). The **control message format** is not explicitly detailed, but it likely follows a **structured protocol** (e.g., JSON, CSV, or binary) transmitted over the same communication channel used for sensor data. **Validation and authentication mechanisms** are not described, but the system implies **implicit trust** due to the closed-loop nature of the incubator. **Feedback systems** are enabled through the **two internal temperature sensors**, which provide closed-loop feedback to the DT for continuous adjustment. **Conflict resolution** is not explicitly addressed, but the DT’s **calibration algorithms** (Document 1, Fig. 1) ensure consistency between the virtual and physical states. **Command execution confirmation** is inferred via the **monitoring loop**, where the DT verifies whether the PT’s behavior aligns with the expected outcomes (e.g., temperature stabilization).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin (DT) of the incubator relies on a **cyber-physical system (CPS) architecture** where the **Raspberry Pi** serves as the central node for data acquisition and control. The **network topology** is **star-shaped**, with the Pi acting as the hub connecting to **temperature sensors** (wired or wireless) and the **heatbed/fan actuators**. The **communication protocols** are unspecified but likely include **serial (UART), I2C, or SPI** for sensor-actuator interfaces, with potential **Wi-Fi/Ethernet** for cloud-edge integration if extended. **Bandwidth requirements** are minimal (low data rates, <10 kbps) due to the periodic nature of temperature readings. **Latency requirements** are critical for control stability, implying **sub-second response times** (e.g., <500 ms for actuator commands). **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** are not detailed, but the system’s simplicity suggests **hardware-level redundancy** (e.g., backup sensors) or **software-based fault tolerance** (e.g., sensor fusion). **Security protocols** are not mentioned, but basic measures like **data encryption** (e.g., TLS for wireless) or **access control** (e.g., Raspberry Pi credentials) may be assumed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The Digital Twin (DT) of the incubator is deployed on a **Raspberry Pi** (edge device), indicating an **on-premise/edge computing model** with minimal cloud dependency. The **computational requirements** are lightweight, focusing on **real-time data processing** (e.g., PID control, calibration algorithms) and **simulation of thermal dynamics** (e.g., differential equation solvers for temperature prediction). **Storage requirements** are minimal, storing only **sensor logs, model parameters, and calibration histories**. **Scalability mechanisms** are not addressed, as the system is designed for a single incubator instance. **Deployment models** are **static** (no dynamic reconfiguration), but the DT’s **reconfigurability** (Document 2) suggests potential for **runtime model updates** (e.g., adjusting thermal parameters for different payloads). **Availability/reliability assurance** is implied through the **closed-loop monitoring** (Document 1, Fig. 1), where deviations trigger corrective actions (e.g., alerting humans or adjusting actuators). The system does not specify **high-availability clustering** or **failover mechanisms**, but the Raspberry Pi’s local deployment ensures **low-latency operation** critical for safety.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle stages in the provided context include **pre-deployment modeling and simulation phases**, where abstract representations (e.g., CAD models for physical structure or differential equation-based dynamic models for behavior) are developed to understand and optimize system aspects. These models are later integrated into the DT framework during deployment, where they serve as foundational components alongside real-time **data acquisition** (via sensors) and **algorithm-driven calibration** (e.g., parameter estimation for Cyber-Physical System (CPS) models). Post-deployment, the DT operates in a **closed-loop feedback system**, where calibrated models inform **monitors** for safety/optimality checks, enabling real-time adjustments to the Physical Twin (PT) via human or algorithmic intervention. The lifecycle also implicitly includes **evolutionary phases**, where models are continuously updated via calibration algorithms to reflect changes in the PT or its environment, ensuring long-term alignment between the DT and PT. Governance is implied through the iterative validation of model accuracy (e.g., via simulation vs. real-world behavior comparisons) and the structured decision-making process for corrective actions when violations are detected (as depicted in Figure 1).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process for the Digital Twin (DT) in the provided context follows a **model-centric, iterative engineering methodology**, beginning with the creation of **multi-fidelity models** (e.g., CAD for geometric properties and differential equation-based models for dynamic behavior) to capture distinct aspects of the Cyber-Physical System (CPS) and its environment. These models are developed at varying levels of abstraction, with a trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency (e.g., slower-to-analyze high-fidelity models for critical dynamics). The process incorporates **calibration algorithms** to refine model parameters using real-time sensor data from the Physical Twin (PT), ensuring alignment between the DT and PT. Quality assurance is implicitly tied to **validation via simulation experiments** (e.g., reproducing PT behavior under current conditions) and **monitoring for safety/optimality violations**, which trigger corrective actions. Version control and evolution management are not explicitly detailed but are inferred through the **continuous calibration loop** (Figure 1), where updated models and data iteratively improve the DT’s predictive and control capabilities. Testing and validation are demonstrated through the **Incubator case study**, where two models with calibration methods are built (Section 3) and experimentally validated (Section 4), though specific testing protocols (e.g., unit/integration tests) are not described.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin (DT) provides **real-time and predictive insights** through a combination of **data-driven analytics** and **model-based simulations**. Insights are generated by **monitors** that analyze calibrated CPS models and sensor data to detect deviations from safe/optimal operating conditions. Decision-making is supported by **algorithmically derived recommendations**, such as parameter adjustments or control actions, which are either executed autonomously or presented to human operators for validation. Confidence measures for recommendations are implied through the **calibration process**, where model accuracy is continuously verified against real-world data (e.g., via residual analysis or simulation-reality comparisons). User interaction is facilitated through **visualizations** (e.g., advanced graphical representations of PT behavior) and **supervisory controllers**, enabling humans to intervene when automated decisions are uncertain or require approval. The Incubator case study (Section 5) hints at broader goals like **predictability, reconfigurability, and reduced maintenance**, though specific analytics techniques (e.g., machine learning, optimization algorithms) are not detailed.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1
block_4_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
